questions.question: Which Liquid expression iterates every tag on the site along with the posts that use it?
choices: `{% for post in site.posts %}{% for tag in post.tags %}...`
`{% for tag in site.tags %}` where `tag[0]` is the name and `tag[1]` is the posts array
questions.explanation: `site.tags` is a hash of tag name → posts array. Liquid iterates it as `[name, posts]` pairs - `tag[0]` is the name, `tag[1]` is the posts. No plugin or data file is required.

questions.question: You have a four-part walkthrough and want each post to show "You are reading 2 of 4" with links to the other parts. Which front-matter pattern fits best?
choices: Add the same tag to all four posts and render a tag index on each one
Put all four posts in a category and use the category permalink
Add a `series:` key (same value on all four) and a `series_order:` integer; build siblings via `where`/`sort`
Hard-code a sidebar HTML snippet on each post
questions.explanation: A series is an ordered, ad-hoc group - tags don't impose order, categories are structural. A `series:` key plus a `series_order:` integer lets a small Liquid filter find and order siblings without plugins.

questions.question: On a Jekyll post, what does `page.next` actually refer to?
choices: The next post in alphabetical order by title
The next post in the same category
The post that comes after this one in `site.posts` - typically chronologically newer
The next page in the site's top-level navigation
questions.explanation: `page.next` and `page.previous` are positions in the `site.posts` array. Since posts are normally ordered by date, `next` is the chronologically newer one. The names don't say "older/newer" - surface explicit labels so readers aren't confused.
